text: Underground concerts are MUCH better than mainstream ones. I went to a mainstream concert with several popular artists and the crowd barely sang, moved, or showed energy. Months earlier I went to a much smaller underground concert where the crowd was intense, there were mosh pits, and everyone knew the words. The smaller show felt much more alive even with far fewer people.
label: analysis
notes: Compares two concert experiences with supporting observations.

text: This is one of those if you know, you know things. You're absolutely right, in my experience. Plus, it's easier to talk to or meet the musicians afterwards.
label: analysis
notes: Agrees but gives a specific reason about musician access.

text: Totally agree. The top shows I've been to were cheap small gigs. The band does not have to compete with crowd noise, you can get close, nothing feels scripted, the musicians seem like they are having fun, and you can talk to them afterwards.
label: analysis
notes: Gives several concrete reasons for preferring small gigs.

text: I'll gladly pay to see established acts like Swans or Laibach. Small no-name bands in front of fifty people are more hit or miss, and as I get older the appeal is lower.
label: analysis
notes: Balances the claim by explaining a counterpoint.

text: Is it still possible to catch a Swans or Laibach show for thirty or forty dollars?
label: personal_reaction

text: It is in Europe.
label: personal_reaction
notes: Short factual reply with no broader reasoning.

text: I think I paid around forty dollars for Swans last year in Detroit.
label: personal_reaction

text: I had two shows this week, one stadium show and one club show. At the stadium I was seated far away from the performers, but at the club show I was close enough to fist bump the band. It was a night and day difference, and the club show felt much more special.
label: analysis
notes: Compares two settings and explains the preference.

text: Exactly, the artist actually came into the crowd and hit me with his dreads. Peak experience.
label: personal_reaction
notes: Mostly describes excitement from a personal moment.

text: What is your definition of underground? If a band tours the entire country, they may not be underground. Underground usually refers to shows that are not formally registered, not just bands that are less popular.
label: analysis
notes: Clarifies a term and gives reasoning.

text: There is a huge network of DIY bands touring small bars and houses. Probably way more than you think.
label: analysis
notes: Makes a corrective claim with context.

text: Some might not call them underground, but they had no real venue and only about sixteen thousand monthly listeners compared with mainstream artists at around five hundred thousand, so I would still call them underground.
label: analysis

text: A show in someone's basement or an unlicensed venue can be the best kind of show. The lack of formal staff and security can create a house party vibe where people relax more.
label: analysis
notes: Explains why informal venues can feel better.

text: I have mostly stopped caring about big music acts that are about fandom. I just want to hear music performed with individual interpretation. The hype adds nothing for my enjoyment.
label: analysis

text: Underground is for real fans and real fans bring energy that cannot be faked. Popular events attract people who just want something to do.
label: hot_take

text: I go out to see tons of underground bands I know very little about. It is cheaper than seeing a mainstream artist just as something to do.
label: analysis
notes: Pushes back with personal evidence and reasoning.

text: If we are including small indie gigs then yes, I agree. I do not really think of bands as underground the same way I think of clubs and dance music, though.
label: analysis

text: Mainstream concerts commonly have huge productions and are spectacles in their own right. Neither is better. They are different.
label: analysis
notes: Counters the claim by comparing different strengths.

text: It is hard to enjoy a concert if the artist looks like an ant. I saw local bands at a smaller venue and the vibe, sound, and audience participation were much better than an arena show where the performer looked tiny.
label: analysis
notes: Uses a concrete comparison to support the point.

text: The best show I have ever seen was D'Angelo and The Vanguard on a big festival stage, but I have also seen great small-stage shows. It depends on how much effort the artist puts into the performance.
label: analysis
notes: Provides a nuanced explanation rather than a simple preference.

text: Underground shows are great, but you cannot make a serious argument based on a sample size of two shows.
label: analysis

text: Underground to me means shows in abandoned buildings, backyards, or under freeways that often end in chaos. Fun times, but I am too old for that now.
label: personal_reaction

text: The best shows I have ever seen have been fifteen dollars in small rooms with enthusiastic crowds. The sound is better, the view is better, and the beers are better. Arena shows are awful and overpriced.
label: analysis
notes: Gives reasons, though the ending is strong.

text: The last arena show I went to was Neil Young with Sonic Youth and it made sense because it was all distortion and feedback. I have the best memories of many club shows.
label: personal_reaction

text: Why do people treat an artist's music as less valuable simply because it is popular among teenage girls? Teenage girls often form deep personal connections with music, so why is that treated as a sign that the music is shallow instead of meaningful?
label: analysis
notes: Raises a structured question and gives reasoning.

text: Many artists with teenage female fanbases were designed and marketed to appeal to that demographic. The issue may be that critics associate those artists with commercial or manufactured music and then confuse the audience with the marketing strategy.
label: analysis
notes: Explains the issue with a clear argument.

text: Duran Duran were dismissed as style over substance because they were attractive and stylish, but that was unfair because members like John Taylor were also strong musicians.
label: analysis
notes: Uses an example to support a point.

text: Sadly, he also had amazing cheekbones. This is frying me.
label: personal_reaction

text: Rock music is youth culture music. Much of it was marketed toward teenagers and young adults, and a lot of it was manufactured for teen boys, but music associated with boys is often framed as rebellious while music associated with girls is framed as safe.
label: analysis

text: The Beatles were absolutely marketed toward teenagers and teenage girls early on, even if they later pushed boundaries musically.
label: analysis

text: Rock and roll was a new medium in the late fifties and early sixties. It was mostly young people who liked it, while adults saw it as a threat, which created a generation gap.
label: analysis

text: It is worth noting that the usual history of rock and roll ignores earlier Black musicians who were already using the term and shaping the style long before the sanitized mainstream version.
label: analysis
notes: Corrects a historical narrative with specific reasoning.

text: People underestimate how many older people lived through the sixties and still hated the Beatles. Many preferred artists like Paul Anka and Glenn Miller.
label: analysis
notes: Uses observed generational differences as evidence.

text: Most of the popular artists of every generation are older than their audience. The Beatles were not Boomers; they belonged to the Silent Generation.
label: analysis
notes: Gives a factual correction and explanation.

text: Teenagers were a new demographic in the fifties because they had more free time and disposable income, which made them easier to market toward.
label: analysis

text: I do not think manipulate is a fair word. Popular moments are not always orchestrated by corporations; sometimes the social and economic conditions are right for a youth movement.
label: analysis
notes: Counters a claim with a more nuanced explanation.

text: If you do not know that Sister Rosetta Tharpe had been playing rock and roll before Chuck Berry and Elvis, you need to educate yourself.
label: hot_take
notes: Strong corrective statement with limited explanation.

text: The Beatles' Hamburg years were before they were signed, and their image was shaped by Epstein more than the label. Their autonomy within the label system is part of why they are considered so influential.
label: analysis
notes: Uses historical details to support a claim.

text: Lennon said they continued to write those songs for the teenage girl audience.
label: analysis

text: I think part of the issue is that there are at least three elements at play: marketability, young people's divisive opinions on pop music, and the fact that people hate teenage girls.
label: analysis

text: Why does the music industry create pop music for teenage girls? Because that demographic has shown the biggest payoff, not because of misandry.
label: analysis

text: The industry has marketed music to teenage boys too, especially hard rock, heavy metal, and alternative rock. The difference is that those artists are sold through rebellion, toughness, authenticity, or musicianship.
label: analysis

text: Rock and rap are pop music for teenage boys. Country too.
label: hot_take

text: Sleep Token are a good example because they were respected by prog metal fans before becoming popular with young women, after which many metalheads began calling them fake or manufactured even though much of the band's sound and image was already present earlier.
label: analysis
notes: Uses a detailed example to support an argument.

text: This is absolute nonsense. Sleep Token were never metal and their old fanbase was just metalcore fans hiding embarrassment under the prog label.
label: hot_take
notes: Strong dismissive claim with limited evidence.

text: It seems like the hate toward boy bands, Justin Bieber, and Britney Spears has cooled down compared with the late nineties through early 2010s.
label: analysis

text: We do not live in a monoculture anymore, so people can avoid pop acts they dislike instead of being forced to hear them on radio, television, or MTV.
label: analysis
notes: Explains why backlash may have changed.

text: True, pop acts are more avoidable than ever unless you work in retail.
label: personal_reaction
notes: Brief agreement with a casual exception.

text: Straight boys once made up a material part of pop fandom, but as they moved into YouTube and gaming, they largely abandoned mainstream pop. Criticizing music they do not engage with does not occur to them.
label: analysis

text: Now instead of saying 'boy bands,' it is 'fuckboy bands.'
label: personal_reaction

text: Backlash against mainstream top forty pop has always existed among music fans who want to feel above the masses. I say that because I was one of those people in high school.
label: analysis

text: I was a teen Bieber hater. The backlash was not because he came from YouTube. We hated his hair and thought it was weird that grown women were obsessed with a little boy.
label: analysis
notes: Gives reasons for a past attitude.

text: Do you live under a rock? Harry, Sabrina, and Olivia definitely get hate.
label: hot_take

text: There may be an assumption that young people are impressionable, naive, easily manipulated, and fickle, which connects with ageist, misogynist, and patriarchal ways of thinking.
label: analysis
notes: Explains a social assumption behind the issue.

text: As a teacher and professional musician, I have seen men in the industry scoff at what teenage girl students like while not doing the same to music young men like. It is absurd.
label: analysis
notes: Uses professional experience to support the claim.

text: Music nerd spaces have long been male dominated, and people who flaunt indie credibility often ignore artists like Ani DiFranco despite her importance to independent music.
label: analysis
notes: Explains a bias in music discussion spaces.

text: Ani DiFranco built an independent career when that was practically unheard of, yet I almost never hear people talk about her.
label: analysis
notes: Supports a point with a specific artist.

text: Teenage boy music is also derided. Mainstream teenage taste is usually bland because artists aiming at the lowest common denominator often choose safe options instead of pushing boundaries.
label: analysis

text: You say mainstream teenager taste is bland, but many iconic bands blew up among teenagers when I was in high school.
label: analysis
notes: Challenges a generalization with a counterexample.

text: Misogyny. It has always been that way. When rock matured and became more consumed by male adult audiences, it became more respectable.
label: analysis
notes: Short but includes a causal explanation.

text: Artists also get hate for having a fanbase of teenage boys, like Playboi Carti and Travis Scott. It may be ageism as much as misogyny.
label: analysis

text: I would not say it is as much. Music for teenage girls receives unreasonable levels of scorn far more often.
label: analysis

text: I have seen Carti and Travis get hate for many things, but not because their fanbases are dudes.
label: analysis
notes: Pushes back with a specific distinction.

text: It is good old-fashioned sexism, and it is not exclusive to music. Movies with large female audiences also get dismissed with terms like chick flicks, and criticism has long been dominated by men.
label: analysis
notes: Connects music to a broader cultural pattern.

text: Genres associated as feminine, like melodramas, romantic comedies, disco, or dance pop, are often dismissed as lightweight and not worthy of serious evaluation.
label: analysis

text: There is more willingness to take risks and accept originality from male artists than female artists, especially before the modern pop star model developed.
label: analysis

text: There has never been a faster route to credibility than sneering at what women or teenage girls like.
label: hot_take
notes: Punchy claim with little support in this sentence.

text: I do not think there is an equivalent to the boy band phenomenon. There are not hot-girl bands for boys that people pretend are high art.
label: analysis

text: Did you ever hear of a little manufactured band called the Spice Girls?
label: hot_take

text: I was shocked to discover that Taylor Swift is close to Michael Jackson in recognition. Not because her music lacks value, but because critics do not seem worshipful of her.
label: analysis

text: Well said. I wonder how many men know rock was invented by a Black queer woman called Sister Rosetta Tharpe.
label: analysis
notes: Adds a historical point to support agreement.

text: Music designed for young teens can feel uncomfortable, especially when adults create it with sexual innuendo. That may explain why some people see it as cheapened, though I do not see it mainly as gendered.
label: analysis

text: The problem is when criticism of manufactured music turns into teenage girls like it, therefore it must be bad. Those are two very different arguments.
label: analysis
notes: Clarifies the boundary between two arguments.

text: I am a thirty-five-year-old man and I love many pop girl artists and am getting into K-pop. There is no shame.
label: personal_reaction

text: The first fans of Star Trek were young women. The franchise would not have continued without fans making art, writing fan fiction, and sending letters after cancellation.
label: analysis
notes: Uses an analogy from another fandom.

text: Countless fandoms are kept alive by fanfiction, fanart, and edits created by the same young women whose tastes are often dismissed as underdeveloped.
label: analysis

text: The critical side of culture has been male dominated while the creative fan expression side has often been female dominated, with surprisingly little overlap.
label: analysis

text: For the same reason that books and films aimed at women are perceived this way.
label: hot_take

text: The pattern is that time has to pass before artists once adored by youth can shed the social baggage of their original fanbase. Elvis, the Beatles, ABBA, Duran Duran, and Britney Spears were all reassessed later.
label: analysis
notes: Uses a list of examples to support a historical pattern.

text: I think you would love the poem When I Say We Are All Teen Girls by Olivia Gatwood. It speaks to this topic and is very good.
label: personal_reaction

text: This is such a lovely recommendation. I read the poem and it genuinely resonated with me.
label: personal_reaction

text: Most likely actual answer: sexism. Deeper answer: if something appeals only to teenage girls and not adult women, people assume there must be a reason.
label: analysis
notes: Short explanation of a possible cause.

text: I get ridiculed online because Twilight is my favorite book series. People dismiss it even though it was one of the biggest cultural events of my lifetime.
label: analysis
notes: Uses a personal example to support the broader issue.

text: People seem reluctant to enjoy certain artists, books, movies, or fandoms once they are associated with teenage girls because liking them risks being mocked or dismissed.
label: analysis
notes: Explains the social effect of the label.

text: I do not think many mature adults would refuse to be a Mitski fan because of the implication. She is clearly talented and not what I associate with typical teen girl music.
label: analysis

text: Probably misogyny. Never hear the same rhetoric for boys despite them also consuming garbage.
label: hot_take
notes: Blunt claim with little support and loaded wording.

text: Guess you have never heard someone talk about Limp Bizkit then.
label: hot_take

text: Stuff made for teenage boys is absolutely derided too.
label: hot_take

text: Music criticism often assumes that teenage girls value looks, outfits, dance routines, and image rather than the audio itself, but music is rarely enjoyed in a vacuum and many respected artists also rely on image and backstory.
label: analysis
notes: Explains the assumption and challenges it.

text: Teenagers care more about looks, likability, and relatability than adults. Some teenage stars are successful mostly because fans have crushes on them.
label: hot_take
notes: Broad claim about teenagers with limited support.

text: Criticism of something women like is not automatically misogyny. Authenticity matters in music, and industry-created acts marketed toward young audiences usually get less respect than artists who make their own way.
label: analysis

text: Teenagers have not listened to music for long, have not developed their own tastes, and are a captive audience, so inauthentic artists marketed to them get less respect.
label: analysis
notes: Explains why teen-targeted music may be dismissed.

text: Name a band that teenage boys are crazy over that is comparable in fame and considered more valuable than a female-favorite teenage pop act. I do not think anyone can.
label: analysis
notes: Uses a challenge to support an argument.

text: The same thing happens in movies. Entertainment aimed at teenage boys is called flashy fun, while entertainment aimed at teenage girls is called dumb or shallow.
label: analysis

text: Teenage boy stuff absolutely gets criticized too.
label: hot_take

text: Fast and Furious is always mocked. It is not accurate to say entertainment for boys avoids criticism.
label: analysis

text: Because a lot of people hate women, especially young women, so they denigrate anything they enjoy.
label: hot_take
notes: Strong claim with no supporting evidence in the sentence.

text: Because teenagers have terrible taste and we expect people at thirty not to express themselves in a way that mostly teenagers relate to.
label: hot_take

text: Music marketed toward teenage girls is no more manufactured than much rock or hip hop. The difference is that rock and rap often have a veneer of authenticity and a predominantly male fanbase, so critics treat them as more serious.
label: analysis
notes: Compares genres and explains perceived authenticity.

text: Teenage girls generally have terrible taste in music. Teenage boys do too, but they are not as large or unified a music-buying demographic.
label: hot_take

text: It is a mix of misogyny and contempt for teenage culture. Female taste is often presumed worse, and intense female fandom gives people more ammunition to dismiss it.
label: analysis

text: Pop music targeted at that specific audience tends to feel formulaic and shallow. Artists like the Jonas Brothers did not bring much new to the scene.
label: analysis
notes: Makes a criticism with a specific example.

text: Teenagers are still unformed and shallow, so anything that primarily appeals to them probably is not trying very hard musically.
label: hot_take
notes: Broad dismissive claim with limited support.

text: Say about Led Zeppelin or Christopher Nolan what people often say about Taylor Swift or chick flicks and adult men will yell at you about it.
label: analysis
notes: Uses comparison to point out a double standard.

text: If an audience is mostly people with underdeveloped tastes and strong emotional responses, it may mean the music is not resonating with others because it simply is not that good.
label: hot_take
notes: Strong generalization about audience and quality.

text: So like all the stuff that immature young men like?
label: hot_take

text: In my opinion, the only downside to listening to a lot of albums and a lot of music is that newer albums can lack the memories and nostalgia attached to songs I heard earlier in life. I may think albums like The Queen Is Dead or Violator are musically superior to old pop songs, but those older songs still feel more meaningful because they are tied to memories.
label: analysis
notes: Explains a music-listening experience using comparison and reasoning.

text: I grew up in the 80s and 90s. We would get new albums and play them a lot, so I have memories from those times associated with those albums. I think it just needs to be heard a lot to make memories.
label: analysis
notes: Gives a clear reason for why music becomes tied to memory.

text: All my best musical memories involve opening a new LP, taking it out of the sleeve, putting it on the turntable, and listening to the full album for the first time.
label: personal_reaction

text: Yes!
label: personal_reaction

text: I think the original post is about music being associated with events, like hearing a song at a school dance, roller rink, or in a first car. Those settings create memories that can make hundreds of songs feel meaningful.
label: analysis
notes: Clarifies the original idea and explains it with examples.

text: The music itself was the memory for me. My siblings had a strong LP collection by the time I got into music, and I would sit in the basement listening for hours to Zeppelin, Floyd, Yes, Genesis, and others.
label: personal_reaction
notes: Mostly personal memory of listening habits.

text: Drummers are the salt of the earth. I would trust a drummer with my girlfriend, but I would not trust my girlfriend alone with my drummer.
label: hot_take
notes: Humorous exaggerated claim with no serious support.

text: New memories while listening to whole albums will form in time. There is no limit to how many memories one can have.
label: analysis

text: In five years, whatever you are listening to now will be associated with memories of now. That is all there is to it.
label: analysis

text: That is interesting because I have had this exact same thought. You have to let albums live with you.
label: analysis
notes: Short but gives a clear rule for how albums gain meaning.

text: Sometimes I feel like I am doing albums a disservice because I only listen once. I may like them, but if they do not click immediately, I often do not return even though they might work better after several listens.
label: analysis
notes: Reflects on listening behavior and why albums may not click.

text: I shudder to think of all the amazing music I would not have fallen in love with if I did not give albums more than one listen. I need at least two or three listens.
label: analysis
notes: Explains a listening rule using personal evidence.

text: The first time I listened to DAMN. I did not understand the hype and thought it was weaker than To Pimp a Butterfly. After several listens, I started appreciating it, and now it is my favorite Kendrick album.
label: analysis
notes: Uses a specific album example to support repeat listening.

text: Most good albums grow on you. I think you know what clicks on a first listen, and then you can put those albums in heavy rotation to form a deeper connection. I do not think there is much point forcing yourself to relisten to music that does not resonate.
label: analysis
notes: Gives a nuanced recommendation with reasoning.

text: My middle school band director said you need to listen to a piece of music twenty times to form an opinion. That may be extreme, but the sentiment feels accurate because some of my favorite albums took several listens to understand.
label: analysis
notes: Supports an argument with a rule and personal experience.

text: You need to allow more time for what it means to listen to an album. The fast nature of music now makes people think they should fall in love with music on the first listen, but albums often need time and context.
label: analysis
notes: Develops an argument about streaming-era listening.

text: I totally agree and think this is especially common in the streaming era. I gave music more chances when it physically took up space in my collection.
label: analysis
notes: Explains why physical ownership changed listening behavior.

text: This is one of the reasons I buy concert tickets. I study for the test. If I know I like the performer and have a deadline, I will learn the new songs.
label: analysis
notes: Explains a strategy for connecting with music.

text: I really enjoy going to concerts, but they have some problems. They are expensive and I do not have much money, and many of my favorite bands do not exist anymore.
label: personal_reaction

text: I completely disagree with the popular take here. If you give something a fair chance and it does not click, you should move on and maybe return later. There is too much amazing music to waste time forcing yourself to like something.
label: analysis

text: This just sounds like getting older. Many people approaching their thirties stop evolving their music taste and listen to old favorites for the rest of their lives. It happened to me too, but I got past it.
label: analysis
notes: Explains the issue as aging and changing habits.

text: I once thought listening to one new album every day for a year would be daunting, but now I average seven to ten new albums or EPs every day. My younger self would not believe it.
label: personal_reaction

text: Listening to so much music can make it hard to remember everything, but I would rather hear as much music as possible than miss out on great albums. It may not be the healthiest approach, but it feels like the lesser of two evils.
label: analysis
notes: Reflects on a tradeoff and explains the reasoning.

text: The albums I bought with my own money became more meaningful because I played them constantly. After switching to Spotify, I listened to many more albums but fewer times each, so I did not build the same emotional bond.
label: analysis
notes: Compares physical/digital listening habits with reasoning.

text: Too much access and too many options make it difficult to develop deep attachment because I am constantly trying to keep up with new artists and albums.
label: analysis

text: Listening to an album just to tick it off a list is completely different from listening to an album because you like it and want to hear it at that moment.
label: analysis

text: I do not listen to albums just to tick them off. There are so many artists and albums I want to hear that I often choose something new instead of repeating something I already know.
label: analysis

text: I was born in 1960 and had the luxury of a turntable and a lot of time with great albums. Albums were experiences that artists, producers, and engineers worked on, and even weaker releases usually had enough good music to be compelling.
label: analysis
notes: Uses generational experience to explain album listening.

text: Then along came disco.
label: hot_take
notes: Brief dismissive punchline with no explanation.

text: There is a big difference between appreciation and love of music. Appreciation is trained and tied to knowledge, but to love a piece of music you have to let it into your life and give it time, space, and experience.
label: analysis
notes: Defines a conceptual distinction with explanation.

text: Thank you for articulating this.
label: personal_reaction

text: Persona bands or first person satire about terrible people. The author argues music rarely adopts the perspective of terrible fictional people compared with books or films and wonders whether listeners assume lyrics reflect the artist's real beliefs.
label: analysis

text: It's quite common in extreme metal where songs retell the actions of cruel rulers from a first-person perspective.
label: analysis

text: Death metal often tells horror stories from the killer's perspective. Depiction is not endorsement.
label: analysis

text: Many grindcore and metal bands write from the perspective of serial killers or the ruling class without endorsing them.
label: analysis

text: I don't think your thesis holds because there are many songs from morally compromised narrators like Psycho Killer and Hey Joe.
label: analysis

text: I listen to many bands with villainous narratives. The artform lends itself to melodrama.
label: personal_reaction

text: Randy Newman wrote many songs from awful personas.
label: analysis

text: Pumped Up Kicks is a good recent example.
label: analysis

text: Songs like Tyler, Possum Kingdom and Stan fit this idea too.
label: analysis

text: Big Black is a good example. Audiences often expect singers to express themselves rather than fictional characters, making persona songs less common.

text: Steely Dan's Everyone's Gone to the Movies explores uncomfortable characters. Art lets people explore unacceptable thoughts.
label: analysis

text: Steely Dan's catalog is largely about losers and addicts.

text: Lou Reed wanted rock music to tackle darker subjects and wrote songs about drugs and taboo topics.
label: analysis

text: Lou Reed's Berlin and Suicide's Frankie Teardrop fit the idea of terrible people or dark narratives.
label: analysis

text: Nine Inch Nails might fit what you're looking for.
label: personal_reaction

text: Warren Zevon built a career on this. Listeners often mistake the narrator for the artist.
label: analysis

text: Books are more self-contained than albums, making fictional personas easier to sustain in literature.

text: Amigo the Devil excels at dark murder ballads and black humor.
label: personal_reaction

text: Marilyn Manson may fit this idea. Sorry people are being mean to you.
label: personal_reaction

text: Horrorcore sometimes succeeds when atmosphere matches violent subject matter.
label: analysis

text: You misunderstood Lolita and overlooked many songs that already do this.

text: The Killers have a murder trilogy told from the perspective of a killer.
label: analysis

text: In Germany before the war... is another example.
label: analysis

text: Uncle Ernie and Cousin Kevin from Tommy are examples.
label: analysis

text: Phone-free gigs can improve engagement because people focus on the performance instead of recording it.
label: analysis

text: Jack White's phone-free shows were great because people experienced the concert instead of social media.
label: analysis

text: Phone bans can still be useful even if some recordings help artists go viral.
label: analysis

text: I use my phone as binoculars at stadium shows and record only short clips.
label: personal_reaction

text: People filming the whole concert takes everyone out of the moment.
label: analysis

text: Closing your eyes at concerts can improve focus on the music.
label: analysis

text: Phone-free concerts are a great idea because everyone is engaged with the artist.
label: hot_take

text: No phones on dance floors sounds like a great idea.
label: personal_reaction

text: Recordings are valuable because fans around the world may never see the band live.
label: analysis

text: I cannot really complain about phones because I enjoy watching those recordings later.
label: analysis

text: Recording entire concerts ruins the experience for people behind you.
label: analysis

text: Ghost's phone-free shows were a much better experience.
label: personal_reaction

text: The audience and performers were more engaged without phones.
label: analysis

text: People should learn to experience art without their phones again.
label: hot_take

text: Phone bans slowed entry when I saw Bob Dylan.
label: personal_reaction

text: Artists should be able to request no-phone policies.
label: analysis

text: No phones on dance floors work well because expectations are clear.
label: analysis

text: Phone-free crowds create a better atmosphere for performers.
label: analysis

text: Phone-free shows are better for the audience but worse for artist promotion.
label: analysis

text: I like taking a couple of short clips but not recording everything.
label: personal_reaction

text: Locking phones away is too much because people may genuinely need them.
label: analysis

text: Yondr bags work well in small venues.
label: analysis

text: Seeing almost no phones in a crowd looks amazing.
label: personal_reaction

text: Watching concerts through someone else's phone ruins the vibe.
label: analysis

text: The no-phone idea becomes less appealing when tickets are expensive.
label: analysis

text: Phone-free policies feel arrogant.
label: hot_take

text: What did people do before mobile phones?
label: hot_take

text: Phone-free works in small clubs but not giant arenas.
label: analysis

text: People holding phones overhead are annoying because they block the view.

text: I do not care about phone bans because I usually stand at the barricade.
